class_schedule.topic: What Is a Game? History and Definitions
learning_objectives: Define what constitutes a game versus other interactive media
Trace the evolution of games from ancient board games to modern digital experiences
Identify the core elements present in all games
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 1-2
Rules of Play by Salen & Zimmerman, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to Game Design. In this course you will learn how games are conceived, designed, and tested, building the foundational vocabulary and analytical skills every game designer needs.
key_concepts: Definitions of 'game' from Huizinga, Caillois, Costikyan, and Schell
The evolution of games: Senet, Go, Chess, Monopoly, Pong, and beyond
Core elements: rules, goals, feedback, voluntary participation
Games as systems of interlocking parts
The role of the game designer versus other development roles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of three games (Checkers, Tetris, Minecraft) to identify shared structural elements
setup_requirements: Notebook or digital document for design journal
Access to at least two different games for analysis

review.value: A system in which players engage in an artificial conflict, defined by rules, that results in a quantifiable outcome
Rules, goals, feedback systems, and voluntary participation form the foundation of every game

learning_objectives: Explain the MDA (Mechanics, Dynamics, Aesthetics) framework
Distinguish between mechanics, dynamics, and aesthetics in existing games
Apply the concept of 'fun' as a design lens
readings: MDA: A Formal Approach to Game Design and Game Research (Hunicke, LeBlanc, Zubek)
A Theory of Fun for Game Design by Raph Koster, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Designing a good game requires structured thinking. Today we introduce frameworks that let us reason about why games work.
key_concepts: MDA framework: Mechanics, Dynamics, Aesthetics
Designer perspective versus player perspective
Eight kinds of fun (Sensation, Fantasy, Narrative, Challenge, Fellowship, Discovery, Expression, Submission)
Iteration as the heart of design
Constraints as creative fuel
lecture_notes.demonstration: MDA deconstruction of Super Mario Bros. and Dark Souls to show how identical mechanics produce different aesthetics

review.value: Mechanics are the rules, Dynamics are the runtime behavior, Aesthetics are the emotional responses evoked in the player
Great games emerge from cycles of design, prototype, playtest, and revision rather than a single brilliant plan

learning_objectives: Identify intrinsic and extrinsic motivators in games
Apply Bartle's player taxonomy to design decisions
Explain flow theory and its relevance to game difficulty
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 8-9
Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience by Csikszentmihalyi, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding why people play is just as important as understanding how games work. Today we examine the psychology that underpins player engagement.
key_concepts: Intrinsic motivation: autonomy, competence, relatedness (Self-Determination Theory)
Extrinsic motivation: points, badges, leaderboards and their limitations
Bartle's taxonomy: Achievers, Explorers, Socializers, Killers
Csikszentmihalyi's flow channel: balancing challenge and skill
The danger of over-rewarding: the overjustification effect
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping player motivation profiles for World of Warcraft, Animal Crossing, and competitive Counter-Strike
setup_requirements: Access to Bartle's Player Type quiz online

review.value: Occurs when challenge level closely matches player skill, producing deep engagement; too easy causes boredom, too hard causes anxiety
Four player types (Achievers, Explorers, Socializers, Killers) each seek different experiences from the same game

learning_objectives: Classify games by genre and identify defining genre conventions
Analyze how genre expectations shape player behavior
Evaluate genre-blending as a design strategy
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 3
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 16

lecture_notes.introduction: Genre provides a shared vocabulary between designers and players. Understanding genre conventions helps you either satisfy or subvert expectations deliberately.
key_concepts: Major game genres: Action, Adventure, RPG, Strategy, Simulation, Puzzle, Sports
Genre conventions as implicit contracts with the player
Hybrid genres: Roguelite, Action-RPG, Puzzle-Platformer
Platform affordances and how they shape genre (mobile vs. console vs. PC)
The lifecycle of a genre: innovation, refinement, saturation, reinvention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Genre mapping exercise: placing 20 well-known titles on a genre-convention matrix and identifying outliers

review.value: Established design patterns within a genre that set player expectations, such as health bars in action games or turn order in strategy games
Combining mechanics from multiple genres to create novel experiences, as seen in games like Hades (roguelite + action-RPG) or Baba Is You (puzzle + programming)

learning_objectives: Build a paper prototype of a simple game mechanic within 30 minutes
Identify which design questions a paper prototype can and cannot answer
Apply rapid iteration to refine a core mechanic
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 8
Challenges for Game Designers by Brenda Brathwaite, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: The fastest way to test an idea is with paper and pencil. Paper prototyping strips a design down to its core mechanic and lets you iterate before writing a single line of code.
key_concepts: Why prototype on paper: speed, cost, focus on mechanics over aesthetics
Materials: index cards, dice, tokens, paper grids, sticky notes
The Wizard-of-Oz technique for simulating digital systems
Identifying the core mechanic and isolating it for testing
Iterating in real-time: play, observe, adjust, replay
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live construction of a paper prototype for a resource-management mechanic, followed by a 10-minute playtest with volunteers
setup_requirements: Index cards, dice, tokens, scissors, markers

review.value: A low-fidelity, physical representation of a game mechanic used to test and refine design ideas before committing to digital development
Stripping a design to its single most important interaction to validate whether that interaction is inherently engaging

class_schedule.topic: Playtesting Methods and the Game Design Document
learning_objectives: Plan and conduct a structured playtest session
Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative playtest data
Outline the sections and purpose of a game design document
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 9-10
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 24

lecture_notes.introduction: A game that is never tested with real players is a game designed for an audience of one. Today we cover how to run effective playtests and how to capture your design in a living document.
key_concepts: Types of playtests: self-testing, focus groups, usability testing, quality assurance
Writing neutral playtest questions that avoid leading the tester
Observing behavior versus listening to feedback
Quantitative metrics: completion rate, time-on-task, error frequency
GDD structure: overview, mechanics, levels, art direction, technical requirements, project timeline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Facilitated playtest of student paper prototypes from previous weeks, followed by group debrief on findings

review.value: Systematic observation of real players interacting with a game to identify design problems, validate assumptions, and gather data for iteration
A living reference document that communicates the game's vision, mechanics, content, and scope to all team members throughout development

quizzes.topic: Game Definitions, Design Frameworks, and Player Psychology

questions.question: Using the MDA framework, explain how the same mechanic (limited inventory space) creates different aesthetics in a survival horror game versus a cozy farming simulation.
questions.answer: In survival horror, limited inventory creates tension and difficult resource-management decisions (aesthetics of Challenge and Submission), forcing players to choose between weapons and healing items. In a cozy farming sim, the same mechanic creates a gentle prioritization puzzle about which crops or gifts to carry (aesthetics of Discovery and Expression), encouraging strategic planning without life-or-death pressure. The dynamics differ because surrounding mechanics (combat danger vs. relaxed timers) change the emotional context.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies contrasting aesthetics for each genre
Explains how surrounding mechanics alter the dynamic

questions.question: Define Csikszentmihalyi's concept of 'flow' and describe two specific design techniques a game designer can use to keep players in the flow channel.
questions.answer: Flow is a state of deep, focused engagement that occurs when the level of challenge closely matches the player's skill level. Technique 1: Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA), where the game monitors player performance and scales enemy health, damage, or puzzle complexity in real time to prevent both boredom and frustration. Technique 2: Layered challenge, where the core path is accessible to average players while optional objectives (collectibles, speed-run targets, harder difficulty modes) provide increased challenge for skilled players.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of flow with challenge-skill balance
First technique is specific and practical
Second technique is specific and practical

questions.question: A playtest reveals that 80% of testers quit your puzzle game at Level 3. List three specific hypotheses for why this might happen and describe one data-gathering method for each hypothesis.
questions.answer: Hypothesis 1: The difficulty spike between Level 2 and Level 3 is too steep. Method: Record completion times for each level and graph the difficulty curve; a sharp time increase at Level 3 confirms the spike. Hypothesis 2: The puzzle mechanic introduced in Level 3 is poorly tutorialized. Method: Conduct think-aloud playtests and note where players express confusion or attempt incorrect solutions repeatedly. Hypothesis 3: Players lose interest because the core mechanic feels repetitive by Level 3. Method: Post-session survey asking players to rate enjoyment per level and state reasons for stopping.

rubric.key: Each method matches its hypothesis logically
Methods include a mix of qualitative and quantitative approaches

questions.question: Explain what 'genre conventions' are and give an example of a game that deliberately breaks a convention of its genre to create a novel player experience.
questions.answer: Genre conventions are the established design patterns and player expectations associated with a particular genre, such as regenerating health in modern FPS games or random loot drops in action-RPGs. Example: Undertale breaks the RPG convention that enemies must be defeated in combat by allowing every encounter to be resolved peacefully through a unique 'ACT' and 'MERCY' system. This subversion makes the player question the violence typically taken for granted in RPGs and creates a novel emotional experience centered on empathy.

rubric.key: Explains how the break creates a novel experience

major_assignments.title: Paper Prototype and Playtest Report
major_assignments.description: Design an original game mechanic, build a paper prototype, conduct at least three structured playtest sessions with different groups of testers, iterate on the design between sessions, and submit a comprehensive report documenting the process and findings.
objectives: Demonstrate the ability to isolate and prototype a core game mechanic
Conduct structured playtests and gather both qualitative and quantitative data
Show evidence of meaningful iteration driven by playtest findings
Communicate design intent and process clearly in written form
deliverables: Physical paper prototype or detailed photographs of all prototype components
Playtest observation forms from at least three sessions (minimum 2 testers per session)
Written report (5-7 pages) documenting the original design, playtest findings, iterations made, and final reflection
One-page summary comparing the initial design to the final version with annotated changes

criteria: Core mechanic is clearly identifiable and testable
Prototype components are complete and functional
Rules are unambiguous enough for testers to play without designer intervention
Prototype demonstrates creative thinking
At least three sessions conducted with different tester groups
Observation forms are thorough and capture both behavior and feedback
Neutral facilitation without leading testers
Quantitative data collected where appropriate
Clear changes made between playtest sessions
Changes are justified by specific playtest findings
At least two meaningful iterations documented
Final version shows measurable improvement over initial version
Professional writing and formatting
Clear articulation of design goals and rationale
Honest reflection on what worked and what did not
Proper structure with introduction, methods, findings, and conclusion
Original and engaging core mechanic
Rigorous and well-documented playtest process
Evidence of meaningful, data-driven iteration
Professional-quality written report with honest reflection

participation_and_design_journal.description: Active engagement in class discussions, in-class exercises, and maintenance of a weekly design journal with game analyses

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of theoretical knowledge covering design frameworks, player psychology, and genre analysis

paper_prototype_project.description: Paper prototype creation, structured playtesting, iterative refinement, and comprehensive written report

game_analysis_essays.description: Two analytical essays applying course frameworks (MDA, flow, player motivation) to published games

final_game_design_document.description: Complete game design document for an original game concept, incorporating all frameworks and principles covered in the course

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. In-class exercises and playtesting cannot be made up. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Extensions may be granted if requested at least 48 hours before the deadline with a valid reason.
course_policies.collaboration: Playtesting naturally involves collaboration. For written assignments and the final GDD, all work must be your own. Cite any external sources, including published games referenced in your analysis.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Submitting another person's design as your own, fabricating playtest data, or plagiarizing written content will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.materials: No specialized software is required. Bring a notebook or laptop for your design journal, and basic prototyping supplies (index cards, dice, markers) for workshop sessions.
